title: Climate Change: Causes, Impacts, and Action Climate change is the long-term shift in Earth’s temp

slides.speaker_note: Introduce climate change as a long-term shift in temperature, weather patterns, and climate systems. Emphasize that current warming is mainly caused by human greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuels, industry, transport, agriculture, and land-use change. Use this slide to frame the presentation around causes, impacts, and practical action.

ui.description: A bold cover slide with oversized top title, metadata strip, lower hero image, and right aligned contact information sidebar.

components.description: Full slide dark base color spanning the entire canvas behind all foreground content.
Large centered headline block occupying the upper half with strong condensed typography.
Thin dividing rule and two small metadata labels positioned across the middle of the slide.
Lower left photographic hero area with a small decorative white brand mark overlaid near the bottom.

elements.name: hero_photo
elements.prompt: A realistic wide-angle photograph showing Earth’s changing climate: a split landscape with smokestacks and traffic on one side, dry cracked land and wildfire haze in the middle, and renewable energy with green forests on the other side; dramatic sky, professional documentary style, high contrast, no text.

components.description: Right lower vertical sidebar with coral background and three stacked contact detail blocks separated by rules.

hero_photo.image_prompt: A realistic wide-angle photograph showing Earth’s changing climate: a split landscape with smokestacks and traffic on one side, dry cracked land and wildfire haze in the middle, and renewable energy with green forests on the other side; dramatic sky, professional documentary style, high contrast, no text.

content.__speaker_note__: Introduce climate change as a long-term shift in temperature, weather patterns, and climate systems. Emphasize that current warming is mainly caused by human greenhouse gas emissions from fossil fuels, industry, transport, agriculture, and land-use change. Use this slide to frame the presentation around causes, impacts, and practical action.

slides.speaker_note: This slide explains that climate change is mainly driven by increased greenhouse gases from human activities. Carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide come from energy use, deforestation, livestock, waste, agriculture, and industry. As these gases build up, they trap more heat and push average global temperatures higher over time.

ui.description: A bold asymmetric slide layout with stacked text and a structured detail grid on the left, balanced by a tall image column on the right.

components.description: Full-slide colored backdrop that supports all foreground content and establishes the overall visual theme.
Large heading and supporting paragraph arranged as a compact text block in the upper-left area.

runs.text: Greenhouse gases trap heat in the atmosphere, strengthening the greenhouse effect as emissions from human activity raise global average temperatures over time.

components.description: A two-by-two information grid with repeated icon, label, value, and divider structure under a full-width separator.
A right-side vertical visual column with a small top label, arrow accent, tall image, and logo overlay.

elements.name: tall_image
elements.prompt: Tall vertical educational image showing Earth’s atmosphere with sunlight entering, heat being trapped by greenhouse gases, subtle industrial emissions, forests, livestock, and a warming temperature gradient; clean modern infographic style, realistic colors, no text.

upper_left_text.body_paragraph: **Greenhouse gases** trap heat in the atmosphere, strengthening the greenhouse effect as emissions from human activity raise global average temperatures over time.

tall_image.image_prompt: Tall vertical educational image showing Earth’s atmosphere with sunlight entering, heat being trapped by greenhouse gases, subtle industrial emissions, forests, livestock, and a warming temperature gradient; clean modern infographic style, realistic colors, no text.

content.__speaker_note__: This slide explains that climate change is mainly driven by increased greenhouse gases from human activities. Carbon dioxide, methane, and nitrous oxide come from energy use, deforestation, livestock, waste, agriculture, and industry. As these gases build up, they trap more heat and push average global temperatures higher over time.

slides.speaker_note: This slide summarizes recent evidence of global warming. The 2016–2025 average shows warming of about 1.26°C above the 1850–1900 baseline, with nearly all of that change attributed to human influence. It also highlights January 2026, which was 1.12°C above the 20th-century average and ranked as the fifth-warmest January on record.

header_block.supporting_paragraph: Observed warming reached **1.26°C above 1850–1900 levels** for 2016–2025, while January 2026 ranked as the **fifth-warmest** January recorded.

content.__speaker_note__: This slide summarizes recent evidence of global warming. The 2016–2025 average shows warming of about 1.26°C above the 1850–1900 baseline, with nearly all of that change attributed to human influence. It also highlights January 2026, which was 1.12°C above the 20th-century average and ranked as the fifth-warmest January on record.

slides.speaker_note: This slide summarizes the major climate impacts amplified by climate change. Emphasize that **heatwaves**, heavy rainfall, drought, wildfire risk, melting ice, sea-level rise, and ocean warming are connected pressures. These hazards affect food systems, water supplies, health, infrastructure, ecosystems, and coastal communities, with vulnerable populations often experiencing the earliest and most severe consequences.

ui.description: A bold header slide with introductory copy, four image-and-label cards in a two-column grid, and compact footer metadata.

components.description: Large title and supporting description aligned at the top left with ample white space to the right.

runs.text: Climate change intensifies hazards across people, ecosystems, and infrastructure.

header_block.supporting_copy: **Climate change** intensifies hazards across people, ecosystems, and infrastructure.

card_image.image_prompt: A realistic scene of a city street shimmering under extreme heat, with people seeking shade and a bright sun overhead
A dramatic realistic image of heavy rainfall causing urban flooding, with water covering roads and storm clouds above
A realistic dry landscape with cracked soil, withered crops, and an empty reservoir under a pale sky
A realistic coastal community near rising seawater, with protective barriers, melting ice imagery, and a warming ocean horizon

content.__speaker_note__: This slide summarizes the major climate impacts amplified by climate change. Emphasize that **heatwaves**, heavy rainfall, drought, wildfire risk, melting ice, sea-level rise, and ocean warming are connected pressures. These hazards affect food systems, water supplies, health, infrastructure, ecosystems, and coastal communities, with vulnerable populations often experiencing the earliest and most severe consequences.

slides.speaker_note: This slide highlights the economic and social risks tied to climate change. Emphasize that extreme weather is no longer only an environmental issue; it is damaging infrastructure, agriculture, energy systems, businesses, and households. In 2025, estimated U.S. climate-related costs reached $800 billion, or about 2.6% of GDP.

ui.description: Dark presentation slide with a left title and chart area, right stacked detail rows, and compact footer information.

components.description: Full-slide dark backdrop providing contrast for all foreground content.
Upper-left title area with a large headline and short explanatory copy below it.

runs.text: Homes, roads, crops, energy systems, and businesses face
Climate-linked financial costs reached an estimated

components.description: Bottom row with brand, date, and website text distributed across the slide width.

left_intro_block.intro_copy: Extreme weather is pushing **climate costs** higher across key systems.

detail_item_top.detail_body: Homes, roads, crops, energy systems, and businesses face **rising losses** from severe weather.

detail_item_middle.detail_body: Climate-linked financial costs reached an estimated **$800 billion** in the United States in 2025.

detail_item_bottom.detail_body: That burden equaled about **2.6% of GDP**, showing a growing macroeconomic risk.

content.__speaker_note__: This slide highlights the economic and social risks tied to climate change. Emphasize that extreme weather is no longer only an environmental issue; it is damaging infrastructure, agriculture, energy systems, businesses, and households. In 2025, estimated U.S. climate-related costs reached $800 billion, or about 2.6% of GDP.

slides.speaker_note: This slide defines mitigation as action that reduces the causes of climate change. Emphasize that emissions cuts must happen across multiple systems at once: energy, transport, land use, and industry. The key message is that no single solution is enough; progress comes from combining cleaner power, efficiency, ecosystem protection, methane reduction, and lower-carbon production.

ui.description: A dark presentation layout with a large headline block, horizontal timeline cards, and a full-height photo panel on the right.

components.description: Full slide dark background foundation behind the content and right image panel.
Full-height photographic side panel with a small decorative mark near the lower area.
Large left-aligned title with a supporting paragraph in the upper-left content region.

runs.text: Mitigation focuses on reducing the causes of climate change by lowering greenhouse gas emissions across energy, transport, land use, and industry.

components.description: Four evenly spaced timeline entries below a horizontal rule, each with an icon, step number, and short paragraph.

runs.text: and improve efficiency to reduce fossil fuel use.
Electrify transport and cut methane leaks from energy and agriculture systems.
Protect forests and restore ecosystems that absorb and store carbon.

intro_text_block.intro_paragraph: Mitigation focuses on reducing the causes of climate change by lowering greenhouse gas emissions across energy, transport, land use, and industry.

step_grid.step_body: Expand **renewable energy** and improve efficiency to reduce fossil fuel use.
Electrify transport and cut methane leaks from energy and agriculture systems.
Protect forests and restore ecosystems that absorb and store carbon.
Shift industry toward **lower-carbon** materials, fuels, and production methods.

content.__speaker_note__: This slide defines mitigation as action that reduces the causes of climate change. Emphasize that emissions cuts must happen across multiple systems at once: energy, transport, land use, and industry. The key message is that no single solution is enough; progress comes from combining cleaner power, efficiency, ecosystem protection, methane reduction, and lower-carbon production.

slides.speaker_note: This slide explains adaptation as preparing for climate impacts that are already happening. Emphasize that these actions reduce harm and strengthen resilience across health, infrastructure, agriculture, water, and cities.

ui.description: A dark split-screen layout with a left title and editable table area, plus a full-height photo panel on the right.

runs.text: Preparing communities for climate impacts already underway through practical resilience measures.

left_content_block.supporting_copy: Preparing communities for climate impacts already underway through practical resilience measures.

content.__speaker_note__: This slide explains adaptation as preparing for climate impacts that are already happening. Emphasize that these actions reduce harm and strengthen resilience across health, infrastructure, agriculture, water, and cities.

slides.speaker_note: Close by emphasizing that climate outcomes are still shaped by choices being made now. Coordinated action across governments, businesses, communities, and individuals can reduce emissions faster while adaptation helps protect people, infrastructure, and livelihoods from impacts already underway.
